text:
Incidence of hydrocephalus following decompressive craniectomy for ischaemic stroke: A systematic review and meta-analysis
Christopher Dillon Ovenden a,b , Dwarkesh Dharmendra Barot c,* , Aashray Gupta b,c , Nidhi Aujayeb b , Kayla Nathin b , Joseph Hewitt b , Joshua Kovoor b , Brandon Stretton b , Stephen Bacchi b , Suzanne Edwards b , Lola Kaukas a , Adam J. Wells a,b
a Department of Neurosurgery, Royal Adelaide Hospital, Adelaide, Australia

a Department of Neurosurgery, Royal Adelaide Hospital, Adelaide, Australia
b Discipline of Surgery, University of Adelaide, Adelaide, Australia
c Gold Coast University Hospital, Southport, Australia
Decompressive Craniectomy Malignant Infarction Ischaemic Stroke Hydrocephalus

Ischaemic stroke is a leading contributor to worldwide morbidity and mortality [1]. Life-threatening cerebral edema  with mass effect following middle cerebral artery ischaemic stroke is seen in up to 10% of cases  and  is  termed  a  malignant  infarction  [2].  DC  in  the  setting  of malignant  infarction  demonstrates  an  evident  mortality  benefit  over medical  management  alone  [3 -6].  Common  complications  following this  procedure  include  wound  infection  and  bleeding  [3].

following this  procedure  include  wound  infection  and  bleeding  [3].  Although ventriculomegaly  is  recognised,  the  event  rate  for  hydrocephalus requiring CSF diversion following DC for ischaemic stroke is unclear.

Objective: Decompressive  craniectomy  (DC)  following  malignant  ischaemic  stroke  is  a  potentially  life-saving procedure.  Event  rates  of  ventriculomegaly  following  DC  performed  in  this  setting  remain  poorly  defined. Accordingly, we performed a systematic review to determine the incidence of hydrocephalus and the need for cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) diversion following DC for malignant stroke.

Methods: MEDLINE, EMBASE and Cochrane libraries were searched from database inception to 17 July 2021. Our search  strategy  consisted  of ' Decompressive  Craniectomy ' ,  AND ' Ischaemic  stroke ' ,  AND ' Hydrocephalus ' , along  with  synonyms.  Through  screening  abstracts  and  then  full  texts,  studies  reporting  on  rates  of  ventriculomegaly following DC to treat ischaemic stroke were included for analysis. Event rates were calculated for both of these outcomes. A risk of bias

Event rates were calculated for both of these outcomes. A risk of bias assessment was performed to determine the quality of the included studies. Results: From an initial 1117 articles, 12 were included following full-text screening. All were of retrospective design.  The  12  included  studies  reported  on  677  patients,  with  the  proportion  experiencing  hydrocephalus/ ventriculomegaly being 0.38 (95% CI: 0.24, 0.53). Ten studies incorporating 523 patients provided data on the need for

Ten studies incorporating 523 patients provided data on the need for permanent CSF diversion, with 0.10 (95% CI: 0.07, 0.13) requiring a shunt. The included studies were overall of high methodological quality and rigour.

Conclusion: Though hydrocephalus is relatively common following DC in this clinical setting, only a minority of patients are deemed to require permanent CSF diversion. Clinicians should be aware of the incidence of this complication and counsel patients and families appropriately.

population  is  challenging.  Poor  baseline  neurological  status  due  to stroke can hinder attempts at determining symptoms or signs secondary to hydrocephalus. Definitions of ventriculomegaly are confounded by the parenchymal volume loss commonly seen in large vessel ischaemic strokes, and direct measurements of intracranial pressure are difficult to interpret in a patient with a large craniectomy [7]. Ventriculomegaly is extremely common, with some series reporting it occurs in more than

is extremely common, with some series reporting it occurs in more than half  of  patients  following  procedures  [8,9],  but  clinically  significant ventriculomegaly is much more difficult to quantify.

Diagnosing clinically relevant hydrocephalus in this patient
Of course, not every patient with ventricular dilatation requires CSF diversion. A more clinically relevant measure may be the proportion of patients  requiring  surgical  intervention  for  CSF  diversion.  There  is  a
Abbreviations: CSF, cerebrospinal fluid; CT, computed tomography; DC, decompressive craniectomy.

significant  discrepancy  in  the  literature  regarding  the  proportion  of patients  who  require permanent CSF diversion following DC to treat ischaemic stroke. Waziri and colleagues were among the first to examine this question. They found that two of 11 (18%) patients undergoing DC for treatment of malignant infarction required permanent CSF diversion via  a  ventriculoperitoneal  shunt  [7].  In  contrast,  a  subsequent  study identified no patients that required permanent CSF diversion

study identified no patients that required permanent CSF diversion in a similar population of patients [10]. A slightly larger study published in 2013 reported that rates of hydrocephalus requiring CSF diversion approached 10% [11].

Although DC is a life-saving procedure, hydrocephalus requiring CSF diversion is a recognised complication of decompression for malignant infarction. Event rates of hydrocephalus following DC performed in this setting still need to be better defined, with no previous systematic review examining this question. A more accurate estimation of the risk of the  need  for  CSF  diversion  following  this  procedure  would  enhance informed consent discussions with patients and families being
enhance informed consent discussions with patients and families being considered for this procedure.

A protocol was formulated before the searches for this review were performed that outlined the review question, search strategy, inclusion and exclusion criteria, risk of bias assessment of included studies and the statistical analyses that would be conducted. This protocol was registered  with  PROSPERO  (reference  number  CRD42021228987)  and adhered to the structure dictated by the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) [12] statement along with  the
Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) [12] statement along with  the  Meta-analysis  Of  Observational  Studies  in  Epidemiology (MOOSE) checklist [13].

Varying  definitions  of  hydrocephalus  and  the  overlap  with  ventriculomegaly highlight the challenge of performing a meta-analysis on this  topic.  Some  studies  defined  hydrocephalus  by  combining  serial radiological  ventricular  morphological  changes  and  deteriorating  patient ' s  clinical  condition  [8 -10,14].  Other  studies  solely  relied  on radiological changes with ventricular dilatation with Evans index > 0.3 and narrowed CSF spaces on CT as meeting the criterion for

index > 0.3 and narrowed CSF spaces on CT as meeting the criterion for hydrocephalus [11,15,16]. The remaining studies defined hydrocephalus as elevation in intracranial pressure refractory to medical treatment [7,17, 18].  Despite  the  ambiguity  in  distinguishing  between  hydrocephalus and  ventriculomegaly,  all  the  included  studies  recognised  persistent ventricular dilation with worsening clinical neurological expression and impaired CSF dynamics as an indication for shunt
expression and impaired CSF dynamics as an indication for shunt placement.

Study  inclusion  was  determined  through  the  use  of  a  research question formulated in the P (Population) I (Intervention) C (Comparator)  O  (Outcome)  style.  The  population  was  patients  with  large ischaemic strokes causing significant mass effects. The intervention was DC. Any variation on DC will be accepted (unilateral or bilateral frontotemporo-parietal, bifrontal). The outcome was a requirement for permanent CSF diversion due to hydrocephalus (for example, via a

for permanent CSF diversion due to hydrocephalus (for example, via a ventriculoperitoneal  shunt).  There  was  no  comparator  for  this  study. PubMed, EMBASE and the Cochrane Library databases were searched from inception to 17 July 2021. Reference lists of identified articles were screened to identify any relevant publications. No restriction was placed on the time of study publication. No restriction was placed on the language study being published. Studies were excluded if they examined
the language study being published. Studies were excluded if they examined rates  of  hydrocephalus  following  DC  for  indications  rather  than ischaemic stroke.

The study types included randomised controlled trials and prospective or retrospective cohort studies. The search strategy focused on the following  terms:  Hydrocephalus  OR  Ventriculoperitoneal  Shunt  AND Decompressive  Craniectomy  AND  Ischaemic  Stroke  OR  Malignant Stroke OR Malignant Infarction. Specifically for PubMed (incorporating MEDLINE),  search  terminology  was  ((craniectomy*  OR  shunt*  OR diversion*) AND (hydrocephalus OR ventriculomegaly)) AND (stroke OR  cerebrovascular

AND (hydrocephalus OR ventriculomegaly)) AND (stroke OR  cerebrovascular  OR  ischemi*  OR  ischaemia*  OR  infarct*).  For EMBASE,  the  search  terms  were  Embase  (hydrocephalus  OR  ventriculomegaly) AND (craniectomy OR shunt OR diversion) AND ' cerebrovascular  accident ' .  For  the  Cochrane  Library,  search  terminology was ((craniectom* OR shunt* OR diversion*) AND (hydrocephalus OR ventriculomegaly)) AND (stroke OR cerebrovascular OR ischemi* OR ischaemi* OR infarct*).

Two reviewers (N. A. and K. N.) independently screened identified studies based on title and abstract. Studies included at this step proceeded to full-text  review  by  the  same  two  authors  (independently). Disagreements at each stage were resolved by consensus with the aid of a third reviewer (C. O.). The web-based application Rayyan was used to assist the process of abstract/title and full-text screening [19]. Data from included studies were extracted into a prospectively specified form,

from included studies were extracted into a prospectively specified form, with extracted data including authors, year of publication, country of origin, number of patients, average  age,  side  of  stroke  (right/left),  occluded  vessel,  time  to surgery,  number  developing  ventriculomegaly  (pre-cranioplasty  and post  cranioplasty),  time  to  cranioplasty,  whether  ventriculomegaly improved post cranioplasty, the number requiring CSF diversion procedure, percentage with haemorrhagic

the number requiring CSF diversion procedure, percentage with haemorrhagic transformation or infection as complication and mean follow up duration. The primary outcome was the requirement for permanent CSF diversion to treat hydrocephalus. The secondary outcome was the development of ventriculomegaly/hydrocephalus either defined by the presence of enlarging ventricles on serial  radiographic  measurements,  with  an  Evans  ratio  of  0.3  and without the presence of enlarged subarachnoid
Evans  ratio  of  0.3  and without the presence of enlarged subarachnoid spaces over the cerebral convexities, or via clinical interpretation.

Data  analyses  were  performed  using  Stata  Statistical  Software (Release 15.1 College Station, TX: StataCorp LP). Proportions of patients with ventriculomegaly following DC and proportions requiring permanent  CSF  diversion  were  calculated  using  a  random  effects  metaanalysis.  95%  confidence  intervals  were  calculated.  The  presence  of heterogeneity in the  results  was  tested  for  through  the  use  of  the  I 2 statistic (threshold value of > 50% considered significant

of  the  I 2 statistic (threshold value of > 50% considered significant heterogeneity) and the use of Cochran ' s Q test (p-value of < 0.05 regarded as significant heterogeneity). The presence of publication bias was assessed visually through a funnel plot, and a Begg ' s and Egger ' s test was performed to determine  the  existence  of  slight  study  bias  (p < 0.05  considered significant).

Two reviewers (N. A. and K. N.) independently performed the risk of bias assessment of non-randomised studies of health care interventions using  the  previously  validated  Downs  and  Black  checklist  [20].  This checklist is used to appraise the methodological quality of a research study by considering factors such as the level of data reporting, external validity, bias, confounding and power [20]. The highest possible score is 32, and scores of > 25 were considered excellent, 20 -25 as

score is 32, and scores of > 25 were considered excellent, 20 -25 as good, 15 -19 as  fair  and < 15  as  poor.  Discrepancies  in  scores  were  resolved  by consensus with the aid of a third reviewer (C. O.). Our search did not identify any relevant randomised controlled trials. Still, if present, the risk of bias would have been assessed using the Cochrane Collaboration ' s tool for assessing the risk of bias in randomised trials [21].

Initial searches identified 1206 studies (857 from Medline, 299 from EMBASE and 50 from the Cochrane Library), with 89 duplicates. After removing duplicates, 1117 searches remained for the title and abstract screening.  After  this  step,  1065  studies  were  excluded,  leaving  52 remaining for full-text review. Full-text records could not be retrieved for 9 studies [22 -30]. The remaining 43 texts were reviewed, with 11 excluded  due  to  discussing  the  wrong  population,  one  excluded

11 excluded  due  to  discussing  the  wrong  population,  one  excluded  for reporting on the bad outcome, 17 excluded for reporting on the wrong study type, and two excluded as relevant data could not be extracted. This left 12 studies remaining, which were included. No further pertinent studies were identified from screening reference lists of included studies.  The  search  process  is  detailed  below  in  Fig.  1  (Base  image sourced from Page and colleagues [31]).

Characteristics of the 12 included studies are outlined in Table 1 [7 -11,14,15,17,32,18,33,16]. All 12 studies were retrospective cohort studies, and the continent of origin was Asia in six, North America in three and Europe in three. A total of 677 patients were included, with 417 males (62%). The mean patient age was 55.8 years, and the mean time craniectomy was performed post-ictus for each study was 2.7 days. Patients with MCA occlusions comprised 533 (79%), and those with ICA occlusions

with MCA occlusions comprised 533 (79%), and those with ICA occlusions  comprised  144  (21%).  All  studies  reported  on  rates  of ventriculomegaly, with 30% (257) developing hydrocephalus/ventriculomegaly. Ten studies incorporating 523 patients said that 52 (10%) patients required permanent CSF diversion for hydrocephalus.

Overall, study quality was reasonably high, with an average score of 24.1/32 (76%) on the Downs and checklist. Six studies were of excellent quality, and six were of good quality (See Table 2).
3.4. The proportion of ventriculomegaly/hydrocephalus

Using a Forest plot (Fig. 2), the proportions of patients with ventriculomegaly  were  pooled  across  12  studies  using  a  random  effects meta-analysis model. Heterogeneity in the study estimates was assessed using the I-squared statistic  (96.1%)  and  Cochran ' s Q  P  value ( < 0.0001), which showed much heterogeneity. However, the degree of heterogeneity has been accounted for because a random effects model was used. The overall proportion of patients with hydrocephalus/ventriculomegaly

The overall proportion of patients with hydrocephalus/ventriculomegaly across the studies is 38% (95% CI: 0.24, 0.53). A Funnel plot (Fig. 3) shows no publication bias (all twelve studies are in the funnel). Egger ' s  test  P  value  was 0.024, and Begg ' s  test  P  value  was 0.244 (continuity corrected), so the decision on the presence of smallstudy bias was ambiguous.

Fig. 1. PRISMA flow diagram detailing how study selection was performed.
Table 2 Risk of bias scores as assessed using Downs and Black instrument.
3.5. Proportions of hydrocephalus requiring permanent CSF diversion

3.5. Proportions of hydrocephalus requiring permanent CSF diversion
Using a Forest plot (Fig. 4), the proportions of patients requiring CSF diversion were pooled across ten studies using a random effects metaanalysis  model.  Heterogeneity  in  the  study  estimates  was  assessed using the I-squared statistic (28.8%) and Cochran ' s Q P value (0.18), which  showed  minimal  heterogeneity.  The  overall  proportion  of  patients requiring CSF diversion through shunting across the studies was

10% (95% CI: 0.07, 0.13). A Funnel plot (Fig. 5) shows no publication bias (all ten studies are in the funnel). Egger ' s test P value was 0.145, and Begg ' s was 0.152 (continuity corrected), so small-study bias was not indicated.  Six  studies  had  information  on  the  timing  of  cranioplasty post-craniectomy,  with  a  reported  mean  of  71  (21 GLYPH<0> 108)  days.  No studies delineated the temporal relationship between cranioplasty and improvement in hydrocephalus.

This study examined the occurrence of hydrocephalus after DC for malignant cerebral infarction and found that 38% (95% CI: 0.24, 0.53) of  patients  develop  this  condition,  with  a  10%  (95%  CI:  0.07,  0.13) subset  deemed  to  require  permanent  CSF  diversion.  This  research  is significant  because  it  precisely  quantifies  the  need  for  CSF  diversion following DC, which is a more clinically relevant outcome than ventriculomegaly alone, given the variability in definitions
outcome than ventriculomegaly alone, given the variability in definitions across studies.

Before this study, no systematic review had assessed the need for permanent CSF diversion in cases of hydrocephalus following DC for malignant stroke. Earlier reviews on DC for traumatic brain injury reported hydrocephalus rates of 17.9% [34]. Still, their definition relied solely on radiological evidence of ventricular dilatation, leading to a wide range of reported rates (6 -54%) [35,36]. Similarly, other studies

Fig. 2. Forest plot for proportion of patients with Hydrocephalus/Ventriculomegaly.
Fig. 3. Funnel plot for proportion of patients with Hydrocephalus/ Ventriculomegaly.
reported highly variable rates, ranging from 8% to 92% [8,18], which can be attributed to differing definitions of  hydrocephalus  and ventriculomegaly.

The  discrepancies  in  reported  rates  stem  from  variations  in  how studies  define  hydrocephalus  and  ventriculomegaly.  Patients  may exhibit ventricular dilatation due to factors other than hydrocephalus, such as brain atrophy [37]. Some studies described hydrocephalus based solely on progressive ventricular dilation on CT scans, leading to high reported rates [7,15,38]. Others had stricter criteria, requiring radiological  evidence,  clinical  deterioration,  or  a  lack  of
radiological  evidence,  clinical  deterioration,  or  a  lack  of  neurological improvement [10].

The wide array of definitions impairs the ability to generalise findings.  In  contrast,  the  decision  to  perform  permanent  CSF  diversion, typically  through  ventriculoperitoneal  shunt  placement,  is  based  on clinical  and  radiological  assessments  and  is  a  more  easily  measured outcome.

This study has limitations, as it didn ' t investigate factors associated with increased hydrocephalus risk, which may have affected the results. Factors like the extent of craniectomy [39] and underlying bridging vein decompression have been linked to hydrocephalus [39]. Additionally, the retrospective nature of most studies and relatively small sample sizes sometimes limit the available evidence.

The study ' s strength lies in the methodological quality of included studies.  However,  they  were  all  retrospective,  and  definitions  of  hydrocephalus varied, contributing to statistical heterogeneity. Nevertheless,  focusing  on  the  clinically  relevant  outcome  of  permanent  CSF diversion is a strength. Furthermore, there was no significant publication bias, and only minor bias evidence was found for the proportion of patients developing ventriculomegaly/hydrocephalus.

The study highlights the need for further research to understand the efficacy of CSF diversion in managing persistent hydrocephalus after DC and its impact on functional clinical outcomes, ultimately guiding the management of DC-related hydrocephalus.

This study is the first systematic review to assess the incidence of hydrocephalus  and  permanent  CSF  diversion  after  DC  for  malignant cerebral infarction. Approximately 40% experience hydrocephalus, but only  a  minority  have  been  deemed  to  require  operative  methods  to divert  CSF  permanently.  This  information  aids  informed  discussions with patients and emphasises the need for uniformity in the definition of hydrocephalus  and  the  lack  of  a  standard  decision-making
of hydrocephalus  and  the  lack  of  a  standard  decision-making  tool  for
